speaker: interviewer
text: Two, Jewish art and artifacts. Madame Laurence Levy-Bloch, L-E-V-Y dash B-L-O-C-H, was born in Paris, August 27, 1874. She studied in Marseille. She's painted scenes of everyday Jewish life. And the Baroness Edmond Rothschild collected her. The painting's name is 'Le Cafe Au Lait Du Matin,' the morning coffee.

speaker: interviewee
text: A letter written December 26, 1930 from Levy-Bloch to Schneid and sent from Paris. Page two. This clipping is from January 17, 1930. It's a write-up by Louis Vauxcelles. Called 'Les femmes peintres.' Vauxcelles wrote this very favorable review of Madame Laurence Levy-Bloch's work. Vauxcelle's also the art critic who coined the terms Cubism and Fauvism. Chaim Soutine, S-O-U-T-I-N-E. He was born in 1893 in Smilavichy. He went to Paris in 1911 and died April 1943 in France while hiding from the Nazis. The complete lack of correspondence between him and Dr. Schneid is not surprising, as he-- as he was an aloof, solitary man who considered his Jewish heritage a burden. The image shown is a still life of a duck. The artist Arthur Bryks, B-R-Y-K-S, was born in Poland. He was an artist who painted and sculpted many images of everyday Jewish life. Shown is a clipping from the April 22, 1928, reviewing his exhibition at the Art Center. A letter written in 1931 from Bryks to Schneid. Page two. Page three. Page four. Jean [FRENCH], 1885 to 1930. He worked first in Vienna and Berlin, and was in Paris by the end of 1905. He committed suicide in 1930 at the age of 45 on the opening day of a major exhibition of his paintings. On the left is 'Les Deux Amis,' the two friends. On the right is 'Femme Assise,' the seated woman. Henryk Berlewi, B-E-R-L-E-W-I, was born in Poland in 1894. On the top left is 'La Jeune Fille,' from 1929. On the right is a portrait of Mademoiselle [FRENCH], fille de [FRENCH]. On the bottom left and right are two drawings by Berlewi, and in the center is a photograph of the artist. The sculptor Jacques Lipschitz, L-I-P-S-C-H-I-T-Z, born in Lithuania in 1891, arrived in Paris in 1900. In 1913 he met Picasso and began his association with the Cubists. He left Paris in May of 1940, and reached the US in 1941, where he lived until he died in 1973. Top left is [FRENCH]. 1930. Bottom left is [FRENCH], 1928, '29. And on the right is 'Prometheus' from 1944. A letter from Lipschitz to Schneid written November 18, 1956. Maurice Moses Applebaum, born in Warsaw district in 1890, went to England in 1905, died in 1931 in Upper Silesia. Applebaum is spelt A-P-P-L-E-B-A-U-M. These are six paintings by Applebaum. An article written by Doctor Otto Schneid about Applebaum and published in the Buch and Kunstrevue in January 1931. George Ascher, A-S-C-H-E-R, born in Poland in 1884. He worked and lived in Paris. These are three works by Ascher, painted between 1926 and 1929. A letter written by Ascher to Schneid in 1931. Page two. Simon Muller, M-U-L-L-E-R, born in Krakow in 1885. Shown are two of his landscapes. A letter by Muller to Schneid, written 28th of March, 1935. Page two and three. Regina Mudlak-- M-U-D-L-A-K-- born 1887 in [? Kuleski ?] near Warsaw. From the Warsaw Ghetto, she was sent to Treblinka. Very little of her work remains. Shown is a drawing by Mudlak. A postcard from Mundlak to Schneid, printed in 1928. Another drawing by Regina Mundlak. A correction on the spelling, is M-U-N-D-L-A-K. This postcard from Mundlak to Schneid was from 1929, and is on the back of the image you have just seen. Margonin, M-A-R-G-O-N-I-N, born in Breslau in 1891. A painting of the Jewish cemetery. Arieh Merzer, M-E-R-Z-E-R, born near Warsaw in 1905. The plaque is on the top left. A candleholder is on the right. And on the bottom is a relief entitled [NON-ENGLISH]. A letter from Merzer to Schneid, written August 17, 1931. Page two. A letter by Merzer written November 14, 1933 and sent from Paris. Lou Albert Lasard, L-A-S-A-R-D. These are two paintings from the '20s. A letter with no date. Page two. Page three. A letter written by Lou Albert Lasard, sent October 16, 1956 from Paris. Simone Levy, L-E-V-Y, born in Strasbourg, France, May 29, 1886. These are two paintings by Levy from the '20s. Two letters from Levy to Schneid, written June 3 and 5 1931 and sent from Paris. A letter written April 16, 1936, from Paris. Page two. Isaac Lichtenstein, L-I-C-H-T-E-N-S-T-E-I-N, born in Russia, lived in Poland, and then moved to the US. On the top left is the painting 'La Rue,' or in Yiddish, [YIDDISH]. Next is the photo of the artist. Following that is a self-portrait. On the bottom left is 'The Wedding.' and on the bottom right is a painting called 'The Old Houses.' An undated letter from Lichtenstein to Schneid, written in Yiddish. Abraham Kosloff, K-O-S-L-O-F-F, born near Kiev, 1877. He then went to France. He died in Paris in 1933 of a heart attack. He also had a brother named Benedict who was a sculptor. This painting is from 1927. 'The Abandoned Ones' from 1916. Still life from 1928. A letter from Kosloff to Schneid, written April 23, 1936, sent from Paris. Professor Emil Kunke, spelt K-U-N-K-E, born in Lemberg in 1897. On the top left is Danse Macabre, 1929. On the right is 'Gollum,' 1928. And on the bottom left is [FRENCH], 1929. A letter written March 21, 1931, and sent to Dr. Schneid. Page two. Emil Krausz, K-R-A-U-S-Z. Here are four examples of his art. An article about Emil Krausz from the June 10, 1930 edition of Benjamin Kopman, K-O-P-M-A-N. Born in Vitebsk, Russia 1887, he studied in New York City at the National Academy of Design. On the top left is 'The Animals'; on the top right, 'Animal.' On the bottom, a painting called 'Evening.' 'The Artist with Palette in Hand,' from 1946. A letter from Kopman to Schneid, written June 2, 1931. A review on Kopman by Edward Jewell in the New York Times, February 18, 1931.
